relations.note: Example clinical-data sentence uses the DM abbreviation after first mention.

effect.trigger: first mention of dermatomyositis in a standalone section

quotes: every standalone section must establish `dermatomyositis (DM)` at its own first mention
After first mention of "dermatomyositis," use "DM" in running copy to avoid repetition.
**Do NOT use "DM" in headlines, headings, or subheads** — always the full word there (clarity, accessibility, and SEO on web).
every standalone section must establish `dermatomyositis (DM)` at its own first mention

rules.intent: Ensure clarity, accessibility, and SEO while allowing concise running copy.
rules.rule_text: After the first mention of "dermatomyositis", use "DM" in running copy to avoid repetition, using the first-mention pattern `dermatomyositis (DM)`. Do NOT use "DM" in headlines, headings, or subheads — always use the full word there. Because sections are assembled later, every standalone section must establish `dermatomyositis (DM)` at its own first mention unless the assembly brief states the abbreviation is established upstream.

rules.intent: Respect patients with inclusive, person-first language.
rules.rule_text: In patient materials, use person-first inclusive language: say "someone with dermatomyositis" / "people with dermatomyositis" — not "patients" ("patients" is acceptable only in HCP materials). Use "care team" (not "staff") for the network of patients, HCPs, and support systems.

quotes: spell out zero–nine; numerals for 10+. **Exceptions — always numerals: percentages, ages, ratios.** Percentages use the % sign (e.g., 74%).

rules.rule_text: Spell out numbers zero–nine; use numerals for 10+. Exceptions — always numerals for percentages, ages, and ratios. Percentages use the % sign (e.g., 74%).

quotes: avoid negative or fear-based language; use an empathetic, supportive, trust-building tone without oversimplifying

rules.intent: Present clinical data supportively to build patient trust.
rules.rule_text: When citing clinical data, avoid negative or fear-based language; use an empathetic, supportive, trust-building tone without oversimplifying. Example pattern: "Participants in the VALOR study taking LISRAYA had nearly 50% more improvement in their DM symptoms after one year compared to placebo."

quotes: Oxford/serial comma always in series of 3+.

rules.rule_text: Always use the Oxford/serial comma in series of 3 or more items.
